title: Quiz: Finite Difference Methods
description: Test your understanding of numerical gradient methods

questions.question: What is the error order of central finite differences?
questions.explanation: Central differences have O(ε²) truncation error because the first-order error terms cancel out. This is more accurate than forward/backward differences which have O(ε) error.

questions.question: How many circuit evaluations does SPSA require regardless of parameter count?
questions.explanation: SPSA (Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation) requires only 2 circuit evaluations total, regardless of how many parameters. It perturbs all parameters simultaneously.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off when using SPSA?
questions.explanation: SPSA produces unbiased gradient estimates but with higher variance compared to per-parameter methods. The trade-off is fewer evaluations for noisier estimates.

questions.question: Making the step size ε smaller always improves gradient accuracy.
questions.explanation: False. While smaller ε reduces truncation error, it increases sensitivity to numerical/shot noise. The optimal ε balances these two error sources.

questions.question: For a circuit with shot noise σ, what is the optimal step size order?
questions.explanation: The optimal step size is ε ~ σ^(1/3) for central differences. This balances truncation error O(ε²) with noise amplification O(σ/ε).

questions.question: When should you prefer finite differences over parameter-shift?

options.text: When using RX, RY, RZ gates
When gates don't have simple generator structure

questions.explanation: Finite differences are preferred when gates don't have the simple generator structure required for parameter-shift (like arbitrary unitaries or gates with complex eigenvalue spectra).

questions.question: What is the five-point stencil error order?
questions.explanation: The five-point stencil achieves O(ε⁴) accuracy by using four function evaluations per parameter. It's more accurate than central differences but requires more evaluations.

questions.question: Which are advantages of finite difference methods? (Select all)

options.text: Don't require knowledge of gate structure

questions.explanation: Finite differences work with any differentiable function (a), are easy to implement (c), and don't require gate structure knowledge (d). However, they give approximate, not exact, gradients.
